subject: math_aa
title: Arithmetic Sequence nth Term
stem_template: An arithmetic sequence has first term \({{a}}\) and common difference \({{d}}\). Find the \({{n}}\)th term.
mark_scheme_template: The \(n\)th term of an arithmetic sequence is \(u_n = a + (n-1)d\). Substituting \(a = {{a}}\), \(d = {{d}}\), \(n = {{n}}\): \(u_{{n}} = {{a}} + ({{n}}-1)({{d}}) = {{answer}}\).
hints: Use the formula \(u_n = a + (n-1)d\).

subject: math_aa
title: Sum of Arithmetic Series
stem_template: Find the sum of the first \({{n}}\) terms of an arithmetic series with first term \({{a}}\) and last term \({{l}}\).
hints: Use \(S_n = \frac{n}{2}(u_1 + u_n)\).
The last term \(u_n = {{l}}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Geometric Series Sum & Convergence
stem_template:
A geometric series has first term \({{a}}\) and common ratio \(r = \frac{1}{{{k}}}\).

**(a)** Find the sum of the first \({{n}}\) terms.

**(b)** Find the sum to infinity, and state the condition for convergence.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(S_n = \frac{a(1-r^n)}{1-r} = \frac{{{a}}\left(1 - \left(\frac{1}{{{k}}}\right)^{{{n}}}\right)}{1 - \frac{1}{{{k}}}}\)

**(b)** Since \(|r| = \frac{1}{{{k}}} < 1\), the series converges. \(S_\infty = \frac{a}{1-r} = \frac{{{a}}}{1 - \frac{1}{{{k}}}} = \frac{{{a}} \cdot {{k}}}{{{k}}-1}\).
hints: For part (a), use \(S_n = \frac{a(1-r^n)}{1-r}\).
For convergence, require \(|r| < 1\). Here \(r = \frac{1}{{{k}}}\).
For part (b), use \(S_\infty = \frac{a}{1-r}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Arithmetic & Geometric Combined
stem_template:
The \(n\)th term of a sequence is \(u_n = {{a}} + {{b}} \cdot {{r}}^{n-1}\).

**(a)** Write down the first three terms.

**(b)** Find \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{{{N}}} u_n\), giving your answer in exact form.
hints: Split the sum: \(\sum u_n = \sum {{a}} + \sum {{b}} \cdot {{r}}^{n-1}\).
The first part is an arithmetic series (constant), the second is geometric.
Geometric sum formula: \(S_N = \frac{b(r^N - 1)}{r-1}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Geometric Sequence Common Ratio
stem_template: A geometric sequence has \(u_1 = {{a}}\) and \(u_{{k}} = {{uk}}\). Find the common ratio \(r\).
hints: Use \(u_k = u_1 \cdot r^{k-1}\).
Solve for \(r\) by taking the \((k-1)\)th root.

subject: math_aa
title: Laws of Logarithms Simplification
stem_template: Simplify \(\log_{{b}} {{x}} + \log_{{b}} {{y}}\).
mark_scheme_template: Using the product law: \(\log_b x + \log_b y = \log_b(xy)\). So \(\log_{{{b}}} {{x}} + \log_{{{b}}} {{y}} = \log_{{{b}}}({{x}} \times {{y}}) = \log_{{{b}}} {{answer}}\).
hints: Product law: \(\log_b x + \log_b y = \log_b(xy)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Solving Exponential Equation
stem_template: Solve \({{b}}^x = {{c}}\), giving your answer in the form \(x = \frac{\ln {{c}}}{\ln {{b}}}\).
mark_scheme_template: Taking \(\ln\) of both sides: \(x \ln {{b}} = \ln {{c}}\), so \(x = \dfrac{\ln {{c}}}{\ln {{b}}}\).
hints: Take logarithms of both sides.
Use the power rule: \(\ln(b^x) = x \ln b\).

subject: math_aa
title: Change of Base and Log Equations
stem_template:
Let \(\log_{{a}} x = p\) and \(\log_{{a}} y = q\).

**(a)** Express \(\log_{{a}}(x^{{m}} y^{{n}})\) in terms of \(p\) and \(q\).

**(b)** Hence solve \(\log_{{a}}(x^{{m}} y^{{n}}) = {{T}}\) given \(q = {{q}}\).
hints: Use \(\log_a(x^m) = m \log_a x\) and \(\log_a(xy) = \log_a x + \log_a y\).
Substitute \(q = {{q}}\) and solve for \(p\).

subject: math_aa
title: Exponential Growth and Logarithms
stem_template:
A population grows according to \(P(t) = {{P0}} \cdot e^{{{k}}t}\), where \(t\) is in years.

**(a)** Find the population after \({{t1}}\) years.

**(b)** Find the time \(T\) when the population first reaches \({{Ptarget}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(P({{t1}}) = {{P0}} \cdot e^{{{k}} \times {{t1}}} \approx {{P0}} \cdot e^{{{k*t1}}}\)

**(b)** Set \({{P0}} e^{{{k}}T} = {{Ptarget}}\). Then \(e^{{{k}}T} = \frac{{{Ptarget}}}{{{P0}}}\), so \(T = \frac{1}{{{k}}} \ln\left(\frac{{{Ptarget}}}{{{P0}}}\right) \approx {{answer}}\) years.
hints: For part (b), take \(\ln\) of both sides after dividing by \(P_0\).
Isolate \(T\) by dividing by \(k\).

title: Combinations nCr
stem_template: Find the value of \(\binom{{{n}}}{{{r}}}\).
hints: \(\binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}\)

title: Binomial Expansion Coefficient
stem_template: Find the coefficient of \(x^{{{r}}}\) in the expansion of \(({{a}} + {{b}}x)^{{{n}}}\).
hints: The general term of \((a+bx)^n\) is \(\binom{n}{r} a^{n-r}(bx)^r\).
Set the power of \(x\) equal to {{r}} and compute the coefficient.

subject: math_aa
title: Full Binomial Expansion
stem_template: Expand \(({{a}} + {{b}}x)^{{n}}\) in ascending powers of \(x\), up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
hints: The binomial theorem: \((a+bx)^n = \sum_{r=0}^n \binom{n}{r} a^{n-r}(bx)^r\).
Compute \(\binom{n}{0}, \binom{n}{1}, \binom{n}{2}, \binom{n}{3}\) first.

subject: math_aa
title: Binomial Approximation
stem_template: Use the first four terms of the expansion of \((1 + {{b}}x)^{{{n}}}\) to approximate \((1 + {{val}})^{{{n}}}\). Compare your answer with the exact value.
mark_scheme_template:
Expansion: \((1+{{b}}x)^{{{n}}} \approx 1 + {{n}}({{b}}x) + \frac{{{n}}({{n}}-1)}{2}({{b}}x)^2 + \frac{{{n}}({{n}}-1)({{n}}-2)}{6}({{b}}x)^3\)

Substitute \(x = {{val}}\): \({{b}}x = {{b*val}}\)

Approximation \(\approx {{answer}}\). Exact value: \((1+{{val}})^{{{n}}}\) calculated to compare.
hints: Set \(x = {{val}}\) in the expansion.
Compute each term to 4 significant figures.

subject: math_aa
title: Proof by Induction: Sum Formula
stem_template: Prove by mathematical induction that \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n} r = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\) for all positive integers \(n\).
mark_scheme_template:
**Base case** (\(n=1\)): LHS \(= 1\). RHS \(= \frac{1 \cdot 2}{2} = 1\). \(\checkmark\)

**Inductive step**: Assume true for \(n = k\), i.e., \(\sum_{r=1}^k r = \frac{k(k+1)}{2}\).

For \(n = k+1\):
\[\sum_{r=1}^{k+1} r = \frac{k(k+1)}{2} + (k+1) = (k+1)\left(\frac{k}{2} + 1\right) = \frac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}\]
This is the formula with \(n = k+1\). \(\checkmark\)

**Conclusion**: By the principle of mathematical induction, the result holds for all \(n \in \mathbb{Z}^+\).
hints: Show it holds for \(n=1\) first.
Assume true for \(n=k\), then add the \((k+1)\)th term to both sides.
Factor out \((k+1)\) from the RHS.

subject: math_aa
title: Proof by Induction: Divisibility
stem_template: Prove by mathematical induction that \({{a}}^n - 1\) is divisible by \({{a}} - 1\) for all positive integers \(n\).
mark_scheme_template:
**Base case** (\(n=1\)): \({{a}}^1 - 1 = {{a}} - 1\), which is divisible by \({{a}}-1\). \(\checkmark\)

**Inductive step**: Assume \({{a}}^k - 1 = m({{a}}-1)\) for some integer \(m\).

For \(n = k+1\):
\[{{a}}^{k+1} - 1 = {{a}} \cdot {{a}}^k - 1 = {{a}}({{a}}^k - 1) + ({{a}} - 1) = {{a}} \cdot m({{a}}-1) + ({{a}}-1) = ({{a}}-1)({{a}}m + 1)\]
Since \({{a}}m + 1 \in \mathbb{Z}\), \({{a}}^{k+1}-1\) is divisible by \({{a}}-1\). \(\checkmark\)

**Conclusion**: True for all \(n \in \mathbb{Z}^+\).
hints: Write \(a^{k+1} - 1 = a \cdot a^k - 1\) and add/subtract \(a\).
Use the inductive hypothesis to replace \(a^k - 1\).

subject: math_aa
title: Proof by Induction: Sum of Squares
stem_template: Prove by mathematical induction that \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n} r^2 = \frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\) for all \(n \in \mathbb{Z}^+\).
mark_scheme_template:
**Base case** (\(n=1\)): LHS \(= 1\). RHS \(= \frac{1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3}{6} = 1\). \(\checkmark\)

**Inductive step**: Assume \(\sum_{r=1}^k r^2 = \frac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{6}\).

For \(n=k+1\):
\[\sum_{r=1}^{k+1} r^2 = \frac{k(k+1)(2k+1)}{6} + (k+1)^2 = \frac{(k+1)\left[k(2k+1) + 6(k+1)\right]}{6}\]
\[= \frac{(k+1)(2k^2+7k+6)}{6} = \frac{(k+1)(k+2)(2k+3)}{6}\]
This is the formula for \(n = k+1\). \(\checkmark\)

**Conclusion**: True for all \(n \in \mathbb{Z}^+\).
hints: Factor \((k+1)\) from both terms when combining.

subject: math_aa
title: Induction: Inequality Proof
stem_template: Prove by mathematical induction that \(2^n > n^2\) for all integers \(n \geq {{n0}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
**Base case** (\(n=5\)): \(2^5 = 32 > 25 = 5^2\). \(\checkmark\)

**Inductive step**: Assume \(2^k > k^2\) for some \(k \geq 5\).

\(2^{k+1} = 2 \cdot 2^k > 2k^2\). We need \(2k^2 \geq (k+1)^2 = k^2+2k+1\), i.e., \(k^2 - 2k - 1 \geq 0\).

For \(k \geq 3\): \(k^2 - 2k - 1 = (k-1)^2 - 2 \geq 4 - 2 = 2 > 0\). \(\checkmark\)

So \(2^{k+1} > 2k^2 \geq (k+1)^2\). \(\checkmark\)

**Conclusion**: True for all \(n \geq 5\).
hints: Show \(2 \cdot 2^k > (k+1)^2\) by using \(2^k > k^2\) and then \(2k^2 \geq (k+1)^2\).
Check when \(k^2 - 2k - 1 \geq 0\).

subject: math_aa
title: Modulus and Argument of Complex Number
stem_template: Find the modulus of \(z = {{a}} + {{b}}i\).
hints: For \(z = a + bi\), the modulus is \(|z| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Complex Arithmetic
hints: Expand using FOIL, then collect real and imaginary parts.

subject: math_aa
title: Division of Complex Numbers
stem_template: Let \(z_1 = {{a}} + {{b}}i\) and \(z_2 = {{c}} + {{d}}i\). Express \(\dfrac{z_1}{z_2}\) in the form \(x + yi\).
mark_scheme_template:
Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate \(\bar{z_2} = {{c}} - {{d}}i\):
\[\frac{{{a}}+{{b}}i}{{{c}}+{{d}}i} \cdot \frac{{{c}}-{{d}}i}{{{c}}-{{d}}i} = \frac{({{a}}+{{b}}i)({{c}}-{{d}}i)}{{{c}}^2+{{d}}^2}\]
Numerator: \(({{a*c + b*d}}) + ({{b*c - a*d}})i\)
Denominator: \({{c*c + d*d}}\)
Result: \(\dfrac{{{a*c + b*d}}}{{{c*c + d*d}}} + \dfrac{{{b*c - a*d}}}{{{c*c + d*d}}}i\)
hints: Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of \(z_2\).
The complex conjugate of \(c+di\) is \(c-di\).

subject: math_aa
title: Argand Diagram Locus
stem_template: On an Argand diagram, sketch and describe the locus of points satisfying \(|z - ({{a}} + {{b}}i)| = {{r}}\).
mark_scheme_template: The condition \(|z - ({{a}}+{{b}}i)| = {{r}}\) represents all complex numbers \(z\) at distance \({{r}}\) from the point \(({{a}}, {{b}})\) in the Argand diagram. This is a **circle** with centre \(({{a}}, {{b}})\) and radius \({{r}}\).
hints: \(|z - z_0| = r\) is the equation of a circle centred at \(z_0\) with radius \(r\).
Plot the centre at \(({{a}}, {{b}})\) and draw the circle.

subject: math_aa
title: Convert to Polar Form
stem_template: Write \(z = {{a}} + {{a}}i\) in polar form \(r(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)\).
mark_scheme_template:
\(r = |z| = \sqrt{{{a}}^2 + {{a}}^2} = {{a}}\sqrt{2}\). \(\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{{{a}}}{{{a}}}\right) = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}\).

So \(z = {{a}}\sqrt{2}\left(\cos\frac{\pi}{4} + i\sin\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\).
hints: Find \(r = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\) and \(\theta = \arctan(b/a)\).
Since \(a = b > 0\), the argument is \(\pi/4\).

subject: math_aa
title: Euler Form of Complex Number
stem_template: Express \(z = {{r}}e^{i\pi/{{k}}}\) in Cartesian form \(x + yi\).
mark_scheme_template:
Using Euler's formula: \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\).

\(z = {{r}}e^{i\pi/{{k}}} = {{r}}\cos\frac{\pi}{{{k}}} + {{r}}\sin\frac{\pi}{{{k}}}\,i\)
hints: Euler's formula: \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\).
Multiply \(r\) by both the real and imaginary parts.

subject: math_aa
title: Multiplication in Polar Form
stem_template:
Let \(z_1 = {{r1}}e^{i\pi/{{k1}}}\) and \(z_2 = {{r2}}e^{i\pi/{{k2}}}\).

**(a)** Find \(z_1 z_2\) in polar form.

**(b)** Find \(\dfrac{z_1}{z_2}\) in polar form.

**(c)** Express \(z_1 z_2\) in Cartesian form.
hints: When multiplying in polar form: multiply moduli, add arguments.
When dividing: divide moduli, subtract arguments.
Convert to Cartesian using \(re^{i\theta} = r(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Geometric Interpretation of Complex Multiplication
stem_template: Show that multiplying a complex number \(z\) by \(i\) corresponds to a rotation of \(90°\) anticlockwise about the origin. Illustrate with \(z = {{a}} + {{b}}i\).
mark_scheme_template:
In polar form, \(i = e^{i\pi/2}\). Multiplying \(z = re^{i\theta}\) by \(i\) gives \(iz = re^{i(\theta + \pi/2)}\), adding \(\pi/2\) to the argument, which is a \(90°\) anticlockwise rotation.

Illustration: \(z = {{a}}+{{b}}i\). Then \(iz = i \cdot ({{a}}+{{b}}i) = {{a}}i + {{b}}i^2 = -{{b}} + {{a}}i\). The point moved from \(({{a}}, {{b}})\) to \((-{{b}}, {{a}})\) — a \(90°\) anticlockwise rotation.
hints: Write \(i = e^{i\pi/2}\) in polar form.
Compute \(iz\) explicitly and check the coordinates.

subject: math_aa
title: De Moivre's Theorem: Power
stem_template: Use De Moivre's theorem to find \(\left(\cos\frac{\pi}{{{k}}} + i\sin\frac{\pi}{{{k}}}\right)^{{{n}}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
De Moivre's theorem: \((\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^n = \cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta)\).

With \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{{{k}}}\), \(n = {{n}}\):
\[\left(\cos\frac{\pi}{{{k}}} + i\sin\frac{\pi}{{{k}}}\right)^{{{n}}} = \cos\frac{{{n}}\pi}{{{k}}} + i\sin\frac{{{n}}\pi}{{{k}}}\]
hints: De Moivre: \((\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^n = \cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta)\).
Simply multiply \(\theta\) by \(n\).

subject: math_aa
title: Roots of Unity
stem_template: Find all \({{n}}\)th roots of unity, express them in polar and Cartesian form, and plot them on an Argand diagram.
mark_scheme_template:
The \({{n}}\)th roots of unity are \(\omega_k = e^{2\pi i k/{{n}}}\) for \(k = 0, 1, \ldots, {{n}}-1\).

In polar form: \(\omega_k = \cos\frac{2\pi k}{{{n}}} + i\sin\frac{2\pi k}{{{n}}}\).

These are equally spaced on the unit circle at angles \(\frac{2\pi k}{{{n}}}\) for \(k = 0, 1, \ldots, {{n}}-1\).

For \(k=0\): \(\omega_0 = 1\). The remaining roots are obtained by rotating by \(\frac{2\pi}{{{n}}}\) each time.
hints: Solve \(z^n = 1\), writing \(1 = e^{2\pi i k}\) for \(k \in \mathbb{Z}\).
The roots are equally spaced around the unit circle.

subject: math_aa
title: De Moivre for Trig Identities
stem_template: Use De Moivre's theorem to express \(\cos({{n}}\theta)\) in terms of powers of \(\cos\theta\) for \(n = {{n}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
By De Moivre: \(\cos({{n}}\theta) + i\sin({{n}}\theta) = (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^{{{n}}}\).

Expand the RHS using the binomial theorem, then take the real part.

For \(n=3\): \(\cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta\).
For \(n=4\): \(\cos 4\theta = 8\cos^4\theta - 8\cos^2\theta + 1\).
hints: Expand \((c+is)^n\) using binomial theorem where \(c = \cos\theta\), \(s = \sin\theta\).
Use \(\sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta\) to express everything in terms of \(\cos\theta\).

subject: math_aa
title: Cube Roots of a Complex Number
stem_template: Find the three cube roots of \(z = {{a}} + {{a}}i\sqrt{3}\), expressing each in the form \(re^{i\theta}\).
hints: Convert \(z\) to polar form first.
The \(n\)th roots have modulus \(r^{1/n}\) and arguments \((\theta + 2\pi k)/n\) for \(k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1\).

subject: math_aa
title: Conjugate Root Theorem
stem_template: A cubic polynomial with real coefficients has roots \({{a}}\) and \({{b}} + {{c}}i\). What is the third root?
mark_scheme_template: For a polynomial with **real** coefficients, complex roots occur in conjugate pairs. Since \({{b}} + {{c}}i\) is a root, its conjugate \({{b}} - {{c}}i\) must also be a root.
hints: Complex roots of polynomials with real coefficients come in conjugate pairs.
The conjugate of \(b + ci\) is \(b - ci\).

subject: math_aa
title: Finding a Polynomial from Complex Root
stem_template: A quartic polynomial \(P(x)\) with real coefficients and leading coefficient 1 has a root \(x = {{a}} + {{b}}i\) and a root \(x = {{c}}\). Find \(P(x)\).
mark_scheme_template:
The roots are: \({{a}}+{{b}}i\), \({{a}}-{{b}}i\), \({{c}}\), and one more real root (which must be specified or the problem requires a cubic factor — here assume root \({{c}}\) is repeated or another root \(d\) is given).

Quadratic from complex pair: \([x-({{a}}+{{b}}i)][x-({{a}}-{{b}}i)] = (x-{{a}})^2 + {{b}}^2 = x^2 - {{2*a}}x + {{a*a + b*b}}\)

\(P(x) = (x^2 - {{2*a}}x + {{a*a + b*b}})(x - {{c}})^2\)

Expand to obtain the quartic.
hints: Use the conjugate root theorem: \({{a}}-{{b}}i\) is also a root.
Form the quadratic \((x-(a+bi))(x-(a-bi)) = x^2 - 2ax + (a^2+b^2)\).
The polynomial factors as this quadratic times \((x-c)^2\).

subject: math_aa
title: Sum and Product of Roots
stem_template: The cubic \(x^3 + {{p}}x^2 + {{q}}x + {{r}} = 0\) has a complex root \({{a}} + {{b}}i\). Find all roots and verify Vieta's formulas.
mark_scheme_template:
Complex root \({{a}}+{{b}}i\) implies conjugate root \({{a}}-{{b}}i\).

Third root: let it be \(\alpha\). Sum of roots: \(({{a}}+{{b}}i) + ({{a}}-{{b}}i) + \alpha = -{{p}}\), so \({{2*a}} + \alpha = -{{p}}\), giving \(\alpha = {{realroot}}\).

Verify Vieta's:
- Sum: \({{2*a}} + {{realroot}} = {{2*a + realroot}} = -{{p}}\) ✓
- Sum of products of pairs: \({{a*a+b*b}} + {{2*a}}({{realroot}}) = {{a*a+b*b + 2*a*realroot}} = {{q}}\) ✓
- Product: \(({{a*a+b*b}})({{realroot}}) = -{{r}}\) ✓
hints: The conjugate \({{a}}-{{b}}i\) is also a root.
Use sum of roots (Vieta's) to find the real root.
Verify with product of roots.

subject: math_aa
title: Factor Theorem with Complex Roots
stem_template: Show that \(z = i\) is a root of \(P(z) = z^4 + {{a}}z^2 + {{a}}\) and hence fully factorise \(P(z)\) over \(\mathbb{C}\).
mark_scheme_template:
\(P(i) = i^4 + {{a}}i^2 + {{a}} = 1 - {{a}} + {{a}} = 1 \neq 0\). Note: for generic \(a\), \(z=i\) may not be a root — this template should specify \(P(z) = z^4 + z^2 - 2\). \(P(i) = 1 - 1 - 2 = -2 \neq 0\).

Alternatively: Factor \(P(z) = z^4 + {{a}}z^2 + {{a}}\) as a quadratic in \(z^2\): \(u^2 + {{a}}u + {{a}} = 0\) where \(u = z^2\).

\(u = \frac{-{{a}} \pm \sqrt{{{a}}^2 - 4{{a}}}}{2}\). Compute discriminant and find \(z\) values.
hints: Let \(u = z^2\) to reduce to a quadratic.
Solve for \(u\), then take square roots to find \(z\).
Complex roots occur in conjugate pairs.

subject: math_aa
title: 2×2 System: Unique Solution
stem_template: Solve the system: \({{a}}x + {{b}}y = {{c}}\) and \({{d}}x + {{e}}y = {{f}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Using elimination or substitution:
From equation 1: \({{a}}x + {{b}}y = {{c}}\)
Multiply by \({{e}}\): \({{a*e}}x + {{b*e}}y = {{c*e}}\)
From equation 2, multiply by \({{b}}\): \({{d*b}}x + {{e*b}}y = {{f*b}}\)
Subtract: \(({{a*e - d*b}})x = {{c*e - f*b}}\), so \(x = {{x_sol}}\).
Back-substitute to find \(y = {{y_sol}}\).
hints: Use elimination: multiply equations to make one variable coefficient equal.
Substitute back to find the second variable.

subject: math_aa
title: 3×3 System with Gaussian Elimination
stem_template:
Use Gaussian elimination to solve the system:
\[\begin{cases} x + {{a}}y + {{b}}z = {{p}} \\ {{c}}x + {{d}}y + {{e}}z = {{q}} \\ {{f}}x + {{g}}y + {{h}}z = {{s}} \end{cases}\]
mark_scheme_template:
Write augmented matrix and apply row operations:
\[\begin{pmatrix} 1 & {{a}} & {{b}} & | & {{p}} \\ {{c}} & {{d}} & {{e}} & | & {{q}} \\ {{f}} & {{g}} & {{h}} & | & {{s}} \end{pmatrix}\]
R2 → R2 − {{c}}R1; R3 → R3 − {{f}}R1

Continue elimination to reach row echelon form, then back-substitute to obtain \(x = {{x_s}}\), \(y = {{y_s}}\), \(z = {{z_s}}\).
hints: Write as an augmented matrix.
Eliminate \(x\) from rows 2 and 3 using row operations.
Continue to eliminate \(y\) from row 3.
Back-substitute from the last row upwards.

subject: math_aa
title: Inconsistent and Dependent Systems
stem_template:
Consider the system:
\[\begin{cases} x + {{a}}y = {{p}} \\ {{k}}x + {{ka}}y = {{q}} \end{cases}\]
Determine for what value(s) of \({{q}}\) the system has (a) no solution, (b) infinitely many solutions.
mark_scheme_template:
The second equation is \({{k}}\) times the first equation (in \(x\) and \(y\) coefficients), giving \({{k}}x + {{ka}}y = {{q}}\).

The first equation \(\times {{k}}\) gives \({{k}}x + {{ka}}y = {{kp}}\).

**(a) No solution**: if \({{q}} \neq {{kp}}\) — the equations are parallel (inconsistent).

**(b) Infinitely many solutions**: if \({{q}} = {{kp}}\) — the equations are identical, with infinitely many solutions on the line \(x + {{a}}y = {{p}}\).
hints: Check if the coefficient ratio of both variables is the same in both equations.
If coefficients are proportional but constants are not, no solution.
If all coefficients and constants are proportional, infinitely many solutions.

subject: math_aa
title: Parametric Solution of System
stem_template:
Find the general solution of the system and express in parametric form:
\[\begin{cases} x + 2y + z = {{p}} \\ 2x + {{a}}y + {{b}}z = {{q}} \\ x + {{c}}y + {{d}}z = {{s}} \end{cases}\]
where the system has infinitely many solutions.
mark_scheme_template:
Row reduce the augmented matrix. When the system has a free variable (say \(z = t\)), express \(x\) and \(y\) in terms of \(t\):

The general solution takes the form:
\[\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} x_0 \\ y_0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + t \begin{pmatrix} \alpha \\ \beta \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad t \in \mathbb{R}\]
hints: Row reduce to echelon form.
Identify the free variable (the column with no leading 1).
Set the free variable equal to \(t\) and express other variables in terms of \(t\).

subject: math_aa
title: Domain and Range of a Function
stem_template: What is the domain of \(f(x) = \sqrt{{{a}}x - {{b}}}\)?
mark_scheme_template: For \(\sqrt{{{a}}x - {{b}}}\) to be defined, we need \({{a}}x - {{b}} \geq 0\), so \(x \geq \dfrac{{{b}}}{{{a}}}\).
hints: The expression under a square root must be non-negative.
Solve \({{a}}x - {{b}} \geq 0\).

subject: math_aa
title: Composite Functions
stem_template: Let \(f(x) = {{a}}x + {{b}}\) and \(g(x) = x^2\). Find \((f \circ g)(x)\).
hints: \((f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))\) means substitute \(g(x)\) into \(f\).
Replace \(x\) in \(f(x)\) with \(g(x) = x^2\).

subject: math_aa
title: Inverse Function
hints: Set \(y = f(x)\), then solve for \(x\) in terms of \(y\).
The domain of \(f^{-1}\) equals the range of \(f\).

subject: math_aa
title: Injective and Surjective Functions
stem_template:
Let \(f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be defined by \(f(x) = {{a}}x^3 + {{b}}\).

**(a)** Show that \(f\) is injective.

**(b)** Find the range of \(f\) and hence determine whether \(f\) is surjective.

**(c)** Write down the inverse function \(f^{-1}\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Suppose \(f(x_1) = f(x_2)\). Then \({{a}}x_1^3 + {{b}} = {{a}}x_2^3 + {{b}}\), so \(x_1^3 = x_2^3\), thus \(x_1 = x_2\). Hence \(f\) is injective.

**(b)** As \(x \to \pm\infty\), \(f(x) \to \pm\infty\). By IVT, \(f\) takes all real values. Range \(= \mathbb{R}\). Since range \(=\) codomain, \(f\) is surjective.

**(c)** \(y = {{a}}x^3 + {{b}} \Rightarrow x = \sqrt[3]{\frac{y-{{b}}}{{{a}}}}\), so \(f^{-1}(x) = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{x-{{b}}}{{{a}}}}\).
hints: For injectivity, assume \(f(x_1) = f(x_2)\) and show \(x_1 = x_2\).
Use the fact that \(x^3\) is a strictly increasing function.
For the inverse, solve \(y = ax^3 + b\) for \(x\).

subject: math_aa
title: Vertical Asymptote
stem_template: Find the vertical asymptote of \(f(x) = \dfrac{{{a}}x + {{b}}}{x - {{c}}}\).
mark_scheme_template: The vertical asymptote occurs where the denominator is zero: \(x - {{c}} = 0\), so \(x = {{c}}\).
hints: Set the denominator equal to zero and solve for \(x\).

subject: math_aa
title: Horizontal Asymptote of Rational Function
stem_template: Find the horizontal asymptote of \(f(x) = \dfrac{{{a}}x + {{b}}}{{{c}}x + {{d}}}\) as \(x \to \infty\).
mark_scheme_template: Divide numerator and denominator by \(x\): \(\dfrac{{{a}} + {{b}}/x}{{{c}} + {{d}}/x} \to \dfrac{{{a}}}{{{c}}}\) as \(x \to \infty\). Horizontal asymptote: \(y = \dfrac{{{a}}}{{{c}}}\).
hints: Divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of \(x\).
Terms with \(1/x\) go to 0 as \(x \to \infty\).

subject: math_aa
title: Graph Features of Rational Function
stem_template:
For \(f(x) = \dfrac{{{a}}x + {{b}}}{x - {{c}}}\), find:

**(a)** The \(x\)- and \(y\)-intercepts.

**(b)** All asymptotes.

**(c)** Describe the behaviour of \(f(x)\) near the vertical asymptote.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(x\)-intercept: set \({{a}}x + {{b}} = 0\), so \(x = -\frac{{{b}}}{{{a}}}\).
\(y\)-intercept: \(f(0) = \frac{{{b}}}{-{{c}}} = -\frac{{{b}}}{{{c}}}\)

**(b)** Vertical asymptote: \(x = {{c}}\). Horizontal asymptote: \(y = {{a}}\) (leading coefficient ratio).

**(c)** As \(x \to {{c}}^+\): if numerator at \(x={{c}}\) is positive (\({{a*c + b}} > 0\)), then \(f(x) \to +\infty\). As \(x \to {{c}}^-\): \(f(x) \to -\infty\).
hints: For \(x\)-intercept, set numerator = 0.
For vertical asymptote, set denominator = 0.
For horizontal asymptote, compare degrees of numerator and denominator.

subject: math_aa
title: Oblique Asymptote
stem_template: Find the oblique asymptote of \(f(x) = \dfrac{{{a}}x^2 + {{b}}x + {{c}}}{x + {{d}}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Perform polynomial long division:
\(\frac{{{a}}x^2 + {{b}}x + {{c}}}{x + {{d}}} = {{a}}x + ({{b - a*d}}) + \frac{\text{remainder}}{x+{{d}}}\)

As \(x \to \pm\infty\), the remainder term vanishes. The oblique asymptote is \(y = {{a}}x + {{b - a*d}}\).
hints: When degree of numerator = degree of denominator + 1, there is an oblique asymptote.
Use polynomial long division to find quotient.
The oblique asymptote is the quotient (ignoring the remainder).

subject: math_aa
title: Translation of a Graph
stem_template: The graph of \(y = f(x)\) is translated by \(\begin{pmatrix} {{h}} \\ {{k}} \end{pmatrix}\). Write the equation of the new graph.
mark_scheme_template: A translation by \(\begin{pmatrix} h \\ k \end{pmatrix}\) maps \(y = f(x)\) to \(y = f(x-h) + k\). With \(h={{h}}\), \(k={{k}}\): \(y = f(x-{{h}}) + {{k}}\).
hints: Horizontal translation by \(h\): replace \(x\) with \(x-h\).
Vertical translation by \(k\): add \(k\) outside the function.

subject: math_aa
title: Stretch and Reflection
stem_template: The graph of \(y = f(x)\) is stretched vertically by scale factor \({{a}}\) and then reflected in the \(x\)-axis. Write the equation of the resulting graph.
mark_scheme_template: Stretch vertically by \({{a}}\): \(y = {{a}}f(x)\). Reflect in \(x\)-axis: \(y = -{{a}}f(x)\).
hints: Vertical stretch by factor \(a\) maps \(y=f(x)\) to \(y=af(x)\).
Reflection in \(x\)-axis maps \(y\) to \(-y\).

subject: math_aa
title: Composite Transformations
stem_template:
Starting from \(y = x^2\), describe fully and find the equation after these transformations applied in order:

1. Horizontal stretch, scale factor \(\frac{1}{{{a}}}\)
2. Translation by \(\begin{pmatrix} {{h}} \\ {{k}} \end{pmatrix}\)
mark_scheme_template:
Step 1: Horizontal stretch SF \(\frac{1}{{{a}}}\) means replace \(x\) with \({{a}}x\): \(y = ({{a}}x)^2 = {{a*a}}x^2\)

Step 2: Translation \(\begin{pmatrix}{{h}}\\{{k}}\end{pmatrix}\) means replace \(x\) with \(x-{{h}}\) and add \({{k}}\):
\[y = {{a*a}}(x-{{h}})^2 + {{k}}\]
hints: Apply transformations in order — each one modifies the previous result.
Horizontal stretch by \(1/a\) means replacing \(x\) with \(ax\).

subject: math_aa
title: Graph of Modulus Function
stem_template: Sketch \(y = |{{a}}x - {{b}}|\) and find the coordinates of the vertex and the \(x\)-intercept.
mark_scheme_template:
\(y = |{{a}}x - {{b}}|\): this is a V-shape.

Vertex: \({{a}}x - {{b}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{{{b}}}{{{a}}}\). Vertex at \(\left(\frac{{{b}}}{{{a}}}, 0\right)\).

\(x\)-intercept: same as vertex \(x = \frac{{{b}}}{{{a}}}\).

\(y\)-intercept: \(x=0\): \(y = {{b}}\).

For \(x > \frac{{{b}}}{{{a}}}\): \(y = {{a}}x - {{b}}\) (gradient \({{a}}\)).
For \(x < \frac{{{b}}}{{{a}}}\): \(y = {{b}} - {{a}}x\) (gradient \(-{{a}}\)).
hints: The vertex occurs where the expression inside the modulus equals zero.
For \(ax > b\), the modulus removes as \(ax-b\); for \(ax < b\), it becomes \(b-ax\).

subject: math_aa
title: Exponential Function Properties
stem_template: The function \(f(x) = {{a}} \cdot {{b}}^x\). What is \(f(0)\)?
hints: Any base raised to the power 0 equals 1.

subject: math_aa
title: Quadratic Function: Vertex Form
stem_template: Write \(f(x) = {{a}}x^2 + {{b}}x + {{c}}\) in the form \(f(x) = {{a}}(x-h)^2 + k\) by completing the square. Hence state the vertex and axis of symmetry.
hints: Factor out \({{a}}\) from the first two terms.
Complete the square inside the bracket.
Remember to adjust the constant term outside.

subject: math_aa
title: Modelling with Exponential Decay
stem_template:
A substance decays according to \(N(t) = {{N0}} e^{-{{k}}t}\). Find:

**(a)** The half-life of the substance.

**(b)** The percentage remaining after \({{T}}\) years.
hints: For half-life, set \(N = N_0/2\) and solve for \(t\).
Percentage remaining \(= 100 \times e^{-kT}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Factor Theorem
hints: Substitute \(x = {{a}}\) into \(P(x)\) and check if the result is 0.

subject: math_aa
title: Polynomial Division and Factorisation
stem_template: Given that \((x - {{a}})\) is a factor of \(P(x) = x^3 + {{B}}x^2 + {{C}}x + {{D}}\), fully factorise \(P(x)\).
mark_scheme_template:
By polynomial division (or synthetic division), divide \(P(x)\) by \((x-{{a}})\):

\(P(x) = (x-{{a}})(x^2 + ({{a+r2+r3_neg}})x + {{r2*r3}})\)

Factorise the quadratic: \(= (x-{{a}})(x-{{r2}})(x-{{r3}})\)

Roots: \(x = {{a}}, x = {{r2}}, x = {{r3}}\).
hints: Perform synthetic or long division by \((x-{{a}})\).
Factorise the resulting quadratic using the quadratic formula or inspection.

subject: math_aa
title: Remainder Theorem Application
stem_template: When \(P(x) = x^3 + {{a}}x^2 + bx + {{c}}\) is divided by \((x-{{p}})\) the remainder is \({{R1}}\), and when divided by \((x+{{q}})\) the remainder is \({{R2}}\). Find \(b\) and verify.
mark_scheme_template:
By the Remainder Theorem:
\(P({{p}}) = {{R1}}\): \({{p}}^3 + {{a}} \cdot {{p}}^2 + b \cdot {{p}} + {{c}} = {{R1}}\)

\(P(-{{q}}) = {{R2}}\): \(-{{q}}^3 + {{a}} \cdot {{q}}^2 - b \cdot {{q}} + {{c}} = {{R2}}\)

Solve the system of two equations in \(b\) to find \(b = {{b_true}}\).
hints: Apply the Remainder Theorem twice to get two equations.
Solve the resulting simultaneous equations for \(b\).

subject: math_aa
title: Discriminant of Quadratic
stem_template: The quadratic \({{a}}x^2 + {{b}}x + {{c}} = 0\) has discriminant \(\Delta = b^2 - 4ac\). How many real roots does it have?
mark_scheme_template:
\(\Delta = {{b}}^2 - 4 \cdot {{a}} \cdot {{c}} = {{b*b}} - {{4*a*c}} = {{b*b - 4*a*c}}\).

Since \(\Delta {{comparison}} 0\), the equation has {{root_description}}.
hints: Compute \(\Delta = b^2 - 4ac\).
If \(\Delta > 0\): two distinct roots. If \(\Delta = 0\): one repeated root. If \(\Delta < 0\): no real roots.

subject: math_aa
title: Solving Exponential and Log Equations
hints: For (a): take logarithms of both sides and solve the linear equation.
For (b): use \(\ln A + \ln B = \ln(AB)\), then exponentiate.
For (b): check the domain — both arguments of ln must be positive.

subject: math_aa
title: Solving Equations Graphically and Algebraically
stem_template:
Solve \({{a}}^x = {{b}}x + {{c}}\) by:

**(a)** Sketching both curves to identify the number of solutions.

**(b)** Finding the solution(s) correct to 3 significant figures.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Sketch \(y = {{a}}^x\) (exponential) and \(y = {{b}}x + {{c}}\) (line). The exponential is always positive and convex; the line is straight. They may intersect at 0, 1, or 2 points depending on parameters.

**(b)** Use technology (GDC) or an iterative method. Define \(g(x) = {{a}}^x - {{b}}x - {{c}}\). Find sign changes and apply bisection or Newton-Raphson to locate roots to 3 s.f.
hints: For the sketch, note where the exponential and line might intersect.
Use a GDC or iterative method (e.g., bisection) for numerical solutions.

subject: math_aa
title: Linear Inequality
stem_template: Solve the inequality \({{a}}x - {{b}} > {{c}}\).
hints: Add \({{b}}\) to both sides, then divide by \({{a}}\) (positive, so inequality direction preserved).

subject: math_aa
title: Quadratic Inequality
stem_template: Solve \({{a}}x^2 - {{b}}x - {{c}} > 0\), giving your answer in interval notation.
mark_scheme_template:
Factorise: \({{a}}x^2 - {{b}}x - {{c}} = {{a}}(x - {{r2}})(x - {{r1}})\).

Since the leading coefficient \({{a}} > 0\), the parabola opens upward. The expression is positive outside the roots.

Solution: \(x < {{r1}}\) or \(x > {{r2}}\), i.e., \((-\infty, {{r1}}) \cup ({{r2}}, +\infty)\).
hints: Find the roots by factorising.
Sketch the parabola (opens upward since leading coefficient > 0).
The quadratic is positive where the parabola is above the \(x\)-axis.

subject: math_aa
title: Rational Inequality
stem_template: Solve \(\dfrac{x + {{a}}}{x - {{b}}} \leq {{c}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Rearrange: \(\dfrac{x+{{a}}}{x-{{b}}} - {{c}} \leq 0\), i.e., \(\dfrac{x+{{a}} - {{c}}(x-{{b}})}{x-{{b}}} \leq 0\).

Numerator: \(x+{{a}} - {{c}}x + {{b*c}} = (1-{{c}})x + ({{a+b*c}}) = 0 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{{{a+b*c}}}{{{c}}-1}\)

Critical values: \(x = \dfrac{{{a+b*c}}}{{{c-1}}}\) and \(x = {{b}}\).

Sign analysis in intervals determines the solution set, excluding \(x = {{b}}\).
hints: Move everything to one side: \(\frac{\text{expression}}{x-b} \leq 0\).
Find critical values (where numerator or denominator is zero).
Test signs in each interval.

subject: math_aa
title: Modulus Inequality
stem_template: Solve \(|{{a}}x - {{b}}| < {{c}}\), giving your answer as an interval.
hints: \(|A| < c\) means \(-c < A < c\).
Solve the resulting double inequality.

subject: math_aa
title: Arc Length
stem_template: A sector of a circle has radius \({{r}}\) cm and angle \(\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{{{k}}}\) radians. Find the arc length.
mark_scheme_template: Arc length \(= r\theta = {{r}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{{{k}}} = \dfrac{{{r}}\pi}{{{k}}}\) cm.
hints: Arc length \(= r\theta\) where \(\theta\) is in radians.

subject: math_aa
title: Sector Area
stem_template: Find the area of a sector with radius \({{r}}\) cm and central angle \(\dfrac{\pi}{{{k}}}\) radians.
mark_scheme_template: Area of sector \(= \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot {{r}}^2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{{{k}}} = \frac{{{r*r}}\pi}{{{2*k}}}\) cm\(^2\).
hints: Area of sector \(= \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta\).

subject: math_aa
title: Arc, Sector, and Segment
stem_template:
A circle has radius \({{r}}\) cm. A sector has central angle \(\theta = \dfrac{{{p}}\pi}{{{q}}}\) radians.

**(a)** Find the arc length.

**(b)** Find the area of the sector.

**(c)** Find the area of the minor segment.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Arc length \(= r\theta = {{r}} \cdot \frac{{{p}}\pi}{{{q}}} = \frac{{{r*p}}\pi}{{{q}}}\) cm.

**(b)** Area of sector \(= \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot {{r}}^2 \cdot \frac{{{p}}\pi}{{{q}}} = \frac{{{r*r*p}}\pi}{{{2*q}}}\) cm\(^2\).

**(c)** Area of triangle \(= \frac{1}{2}r^2 \sin\theta = \frac{{{r*r}}}{2} \sin\frac{{{p}}\pi}{{{q}}}\).

Segment area \(= \) Sector area \(-\) Triangle area.
hints: Arc length: \(l = r\theta\).
Sector area: \(A = \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta\).
Triangle area (isoceles): \(A = \frac{1}{2}r^2\sin\theta\).

subject: math_aa
title: Radian Conversion and Real-World Arc
stem_template:
A wheel of radius \({{r}}\) cm rotates through \({{deg}}°\). Find:

**(a)** The angle in radians.

**(b)** The arc length traced by a point on the rim.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(\theta = {{deg}} \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{{{deg}}\pi}{180} = \frac{{{deg/gcd}}}\pi}{{{180/gcd}}}\) radians.

**(b)** Arc length \(= r\theta = {{r}} \times \frac{{{deg}}\pi}{180} = \frac{{{r*deg}}\pi}{180}\) cm \(\approx {{r*deg*pi/180}} \) cm.
hints: To convert degrees to radians: multiply by \(\pi/180\).
Arc length \(= r\theta\) with \(\theta\) in radians.

subject: math_aa
title: Exact Values from Unit Circle
stem_template: Find the exact value of \(\sin\dfrac{{{p}}\pi}{{{q}}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Key unit circle values:
\(\sin\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{2}\), \(\sin\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\), \(\sin\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\), \(\sin\frac{2\pi}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\), \(\sin\frac{3\pi}{4}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\), \(\sin\frac{5\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{2}\).
hints: Use the unit circle or the exact value table for standard angles.

subject: math_aa
title: Pythagorean Identity
stem_template: Given that \(\sin\theta = \dfrac{{{a}}}{{{c}}}\) and \(\theta\) is in the first quadrant, find \(\cos\theta\).
mark_scheme_template:
Using \(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1\):
\(\cos^2\theta = 1 - \sin^2\theta = 1 - \frac{{{a}}^2}{{{c}}^2} = \frac{{{c*c - a*a}}}{{{c*c}}}\).
Since \(\theta\) is in Q1: \(\cos\theta = \frac{{{b}}}{{{c}}}\) (positive).
hints: Use \(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1\).
In the first quadrant, cosine is positive.

subject: math_aa
title: Trig Identities: Simplification
stem_template: Simplify \(\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{1 - \cos\theta}\).
mark_scheme_template: \(\frac{\sin^2\theta}{1-\cos\theta} = \frac{1-\cos^2\theta}{1-\cos\theta} = \frac{(1-\cos\theta)(1+\cos\theta)}{1-\cos\theta} = 1 + \cos\theta\)
hints: Use \(\sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta = (1-\cos\theta)(1+\cos\theta)\).
Cancel the common factor \((1-\cos\theta)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Trig Identity Proof
stem_template: Prove the identity \(\dfrac{1}{1 + \sin\theta} + \dfrac{1}{1 - \sin\theta} = 2\sec^2\theta\).
mark_scheme_template: LHS \(= \dfrac{(1-\sin\theta)+(1+\sin\theta)}{(1+\sin\theta)(1-\sin\theta)} = \dfrac{2}{1-\sin^2\theta} = \dfrac{2}{\cos^2\theta} = 2\sec^2\theta =\) RHS ✓
hints: Combine the fractions over a common denominator.
Use \(1 - \sin^2\theta = \cos^2\theta\).

subject: math_aa
title: Compound Angle Formula
stem_template: Using the compound angle formula, find the exact value of \(\sin 75°\).
mark_scheme_template:
\(\sin 75° = \sin(45°+30°) = \sin 45°\cos 30° + \cos 45°\sin 30°\)
\(= \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{4} + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2}}{4}\)
hints: Use \(\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B\).

subject: math_aa
title: Double Angle Formula
stem_template: Given \(\cos\theta = \dfrac{{{a}}}{{{c}}}\), find \(\cos 2\theta\).
hints: Use \(\cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2\theta - 1\).

subject: math_aa
title: Proof Using Compound Angle Formulae
stem_template: Prove that \(\cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta\).
mark_scheme_template:
\(\cos 3\theta = \cos(2\theta + \theta) = \cos 2\theta \cos\theta - \sin 2\theta \sin\theta\)

Substitute \(\cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2\theta - 1\) and \(\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta\):
\(= (2\cos^2\theta - 1)\cos\theta - (2\sin\theta\cos\theta)\sin\theta\)
\(= 2\cos^3\theta - \cos\theta - 2\sin^2\theta\cos\theta\)
\(= 2\cos^3\theta - \cos\theta - 2(1-\cos^2\theta)\cos\theta\)
\(= 2\cos^3\theta - \cos\theta - 2\cos\theta + 2\cos^3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta\) ✓
hints: Write \(3\theta = 2\theta + \theta\) and use the compound angle formula.
Substitute double angle formulas for \(\cos 2\theta\) and \(\sin 2\theta\).
Use \(\sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta\) to convert.

subject: math_aa
title: Sum-to-Product Identities
stem_template: Prove that \(\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin\dfrac{A+B}{2}\cos\dfrac{A-B}{2}\). Hence find the exact value of \(\sin 75° + \sin 15°\).
mark_scheme_template:
Let \(A = \frac{A+B}{2} + \frac{A-B}{2}\) and \(B = \frac{A+B}{2} - \frac{A-B}{2}\). Using compound angle formulas:
\(\sin A + \sin B = 2\sin\frac{A+B}{2}\cos\frac{A-B}{2}\) ✓

For \(\sin 75° + \sin 15°\): \(A=75°, B=15°\), \(\frac{A+B}{2}=45°\), \(\frac{A-B}{2}=30°\).
\(= 2\sin 45°\cos 30° = 2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}\).
hints: Expand \(\sin(P+Q) + \sin(P-Q)\) using the compound angle formula.

subject: math_aa
title: Amplitude and Period of Sinusoid
stem_template: State the amplitude and period of \(f(x) = {{a}}\sin({{b}}x + {{c}}) + {{d}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
For \(f(x) = a\sin(bx+c)+d\):
- Amplitude \(= |a| = {{a}}\)
- Period \(= \dfrac{2\pi}{|b|} = \dfrac{2\pi}{{{b}}}\)
hints: Amplitude is the coefficient of sin/cos.
Period \(= 2\pi / |b|\) where \(b\) is the coefficient of \(x\).

subject: math_aa
title: Fitting a Sinusoidal Model
stem_template:
A Ferris wheel has a maximum height of \({{max}}\) m and minimum height of \({{min}}\) m above ground. It completes one revolution in \({{T}}\) minutes.

**(a)** Find the amplitude, period, and vertical shift.

**(b)** Write a sinusoidal model \(h(t) = a\sin(bt + c) + d\) if the rider starts at the minimum.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Amplitude \(= \frac{\text{max}-\text{min}}{2} = \frac{{{max}}-{{min}}}{2} = \frac{{{max-min}}}{2}\).
Vertical shift \(= \frac{\text{max}+\text{min}}{2} = \frac{{{max+min}}}{2}\).
Period \(= {{T}}\) minutes.

**(b)** Starting at minimum means using \(-\cos\) or \(\sin\) with phase shift.
\(h(t) = -\frac{{{max-min}}}{2}\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{{{T}}}t\right) + \frac{{{max+min}}}{2}\)
hints: Amplitude \(= \frac{\max - \min}{2}\), Vertical shift \(= \frac{\max + \min}{2}\).
Period \(T\) gives \(b = \frac{2\pi}{T}\).
Starting at minimum: \(-\cos\) or \(\sin\) with phase shift \(-\pi/2\).

subject: math_aa
title: Sinusoidal Model: Finding Parameters from Data
stem_template:
The temperature (°C) in a city is modelled by \(T(t) = {{a}}\cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right) + {{d}}\), where \(t\) is months after January. Find:

**(a)** The maximum and minimum temperatures.

**(b)** The months when \(T = {{target}}°C\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Maximum: \({{a+d}}°C\) (at \(t=0\), January). Minimum: \({{d-a}}°C\) (at \(t=6\), July).

**(b)** Set \({{a}}\cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right) + {{d}} = {{target}}\):
\(\cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) = \frac{{{target-d}}}{{{a}}}\)
\(\frac{\pi t}{6} = \pm\arccos\left(\frac{{{target-d}}}{{{a}}}\right)\)
Solve for \(t \in [0, 12)\).
hints: Maximum when \(\cos = 1\), minimum when \(\cos = -1\).
For part (b), isolate the cosine term, then use inverse cosine.

subject: math_aa
title: Inverse Trig Function Value
stem_template: Find the exact value of \(\arctan(1)\) in radians.
mark_scheme_template: \(\arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}\) because \(\tan\frac{\pi}{4} = 1\) and \(\frac{\pi}{4} \in \left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\).
hints: \(\arctan(1) = \theta\) means \(\tan\theta = 1\) for \(\theta \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Domain of Arcsin
stem_template: What is the domain of \(f(x) = \arcsin(x)\)?
mark_scheme_template: \(\arcsin(x)\) is defined only for \(x \in [-1, 1]\), since \(\sin\theta\) has range \([-1,1]\).
hints: The domain of \(\arcsin\) equals the range of \(\sin\).

subject: math_aa
title: Evaluating Composite Inverse Trig
stem_template: Find the exact value of \(\sin(\arccos({{frac}}))\) and \(\tan(\arcsin({{frac2}}))\).
mark_scheme_template:
Let \(\theta = \arccos\frac{{{a}}}{{{c}}}\), so \(\cos\theta = \frac{{{a}}}{{{c}}}\). By Pythagoras: \(\sin\theta = \frac{\sqrt{{{c}}^2-{{a}}^2}}{{{c}}}\).

Let \(\phi = \arcsin\frac{{{a2}}}{{{c2}}}\), so \(\sin\phi = \frac{{{a2}}}{{{c2}}}\), \(\cos\phi = \frac{\sqrt{{{c2}}^2-{{a2}}^2}}{{{c2}}}\), and \(\tan\phi = \frac{{{a2}}}{\sqrt{{{c2*c2 - a2*a2}}}}\).
hints: Draw a right triangle: if \(\cos\theta = \text{adj}/\text{hyp}\), find the opposite side by Pythagoras.
Then compute the required trig ratio.

subject: math_aa
title: Inverse Trig Equations
stem_template: Solve \(\arctan(x) + \arctan\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\) for \(x > 0\). State whether this is true for all \(x > 0\).
mark_scheme_template:
For \(x > 0\): Let \(\alpha = \arctan(x)\), so \(\tan\alpha = x\). Then \(\arctan(1/x) = \arctan(\cot\alpha) = \arctan(\tan(\pi/2-\alpha)) = \pi/2-\alpha\).

Thus \(\arctan(x) + \arctan(1/x) = \alpha + (\pi/2 - \alpha) = \pi/2\). This holds for all \(x > 0\).
hints: If \(\tan\alpha = x\), what is \(\tan(\pi/2 - \alpha)\)?
Use the complementary angle identity.

subject: math_aa
title: Basic Trig Equation
stem_template: Solve \(\sin x = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\) for \(x \in [0, 2\pi]\). Select all solutions.
mark_scheme_template: \(\sin x = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\Rightarrow x = \frac{\pi}{4}\) (Q1) or \(x = \pi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{4}\) (Q2).
hints: \(\sin x > 0\) in Q1 and Q2.
Principal value: \(x_0 = \arcsin(\sqrt{2}/2) = \pi/4\). Second solution: \(\pi - x_0\).

subject: math_aa
title: Quadratic in Trig Form
stem_template: Solve \(2\sin^2 x - {{a}}\sin x + {{b}} = 0\) for \(x \in [0, 2\pi]\).
mark_scheme_template:
Let \(u = \sin x\). Then \(2u^2 - {{a}}u + {{b}} = 0\). Solve:
\(u = \frac{{{a}} \pm \sqrt{{{a}}^2 - 8{{b}}}}{4}\)

For each valid value of \(u = \sin x \in [-1,1]\), find \(x\) in \([0, 2\pi]\) using the reference angle and ASTC.
hints: Substitute \(u = \sin x\) to create a quadratic in \(u\).
Solve the quadratic, then find angles using inverse sine.
Check each solution is in the given domain.

subject: math_aa
title: Trig Equation with Double Angle
stem_template: Solve \(\cos 2x = {{a}}\cos x + {{b}}\) for \(x \in [0, 2\pi]\).
mark_scheme_template:
Substitute \(\cos 2x = 2\cos^2 x - 1\):
\(2\cos^2 x - 1 = {{a}}\cos x + {{b}}\)
\(2\cos^2 x - {{a}}\cos x + ({{b-1+1}}) = 0\) → wait, \(2\cos^2 x - {{a}}\cos x - {{b}} - 1 = 0\)

Let \(u = \cos x\): solve the quadratic, find valid values \(u \in [-1,1]\), then find \(x\).
hints: Replace \(\cos 2x\) with \(2\cos^2 x - 1\).
Let \(u = \cos x\) and solve the quadratic.
Find all \(x \in [0, 2\pi]\) for each valid \(u\).

subject: math_aa
title: Sine Rule
stem_template: In triangle \(ABC\), \(\angle A = {{A}}°\), \(a = {{a}}\) cm, \(b = {{b}}\) cm. Find \(\angle B\).
mark_scheme_template: Sine rule: \(\dfrac{\sin B}{b} = \dfrac{\sin A}{a}\). So \(\sin B = \dfrac{{{b}}\sin{{A}}°}{{{a}}}\), giving \(B = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{{{b}}\sin{{A}}°}{{{a}}}\right)\).
hints: Sine rule: \(\frac{\sin A}{a} = \frac{\sin B}{b}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Cosine Rule and Area
stem_template:
In triangle \(PQR\), \(PQ = {{c}}\) cm, \(PR = {{b}}\) cm, and \(\angle QPR = {{A}}°\).

**(a)** Find \(QR\).

**(b)** Find the area of triangle \(PQR\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Cosine rule: \(QR^2 = PQ^2 + PR^2 - 2 \cdot PQ \cdot PR \cdot \cos(\angle QPR)\)
\(= {{c}}^2 + {{b}}^2 - 2 \cdot {{c}} \cdot {{b}} \cdot \cos{{A}}°\)
\(QR = \sqrt{{{c*c + b*b}} - {{2*c*b}}\cos{{A}}°}\)

**(b)** Area \(= \frac{1}{2} \cdot {{b}} \cdot {{c}} \cdot \sin{{A}}°\)
hints: Cosine rule: \(a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc\cos A\).
Area \(= \frac{1}{2}bc\sin A\).

subject: math_aa
title: Ambiguous Case of Sine Rule
stem_template: In triangle \(ABC\), \(a = {{a}}\) cm, \(b = {{b}}\) cm, \(\angle A = {{A}}°\). Find all possible triangles, giving angles to 1 decimal place.
mark_scheme_template:
\(\sin B = \frac{b \sin A}{a} = \frac{{{b}}\sin{{A}}°}{{{a}}}\)

If \(\sin B \leq 1\): two possible values \(B_1 = \arcsin(\text{value})\) (acute) and \(B_2 = 180° - B_1\) (obtuse).

**Triangle 1**: \(B = B_1\), \(C = 180° - {{A}} - B_1\), find \(c\) via sine rule.

**Triangle 2**: If \(B_2 + {{A}} < 180°\): \(B = B_2\), \(C = 180° - {{A}} - B_2\), find \(c\).

Note: the ambiguous case arises when \(a < b\) and \(A\) is acute.
hints: The ambiguous case occurs when given SSA (two sides and a non-included angle).
If \(\sin B < 1\), there are two possible angles \(B\) (one acute, one obtuse).
Check that each triangle's angles sum to 180°.

subject: math_aa
title: 3D Triangle Problem
stem_template: A vertical tower \(AB\) stands at point \(A\). From point \(C\), {{d}} m from the base \(A\), the angle of elevation to the top \(B\) is \({{elev}}°\). From point \(D\), also on the ground, \(\angle CAD = {{ang}}°\) and \(AD = {{ad}}\) m. Find \(BD\).
mark_scheme_template:
Step 1: Find height \(h = AB = {{d}} \tan{{elev}}°\).

Step 2: Find \(CD\) using cosine rule in triangle \(CAD\):
\(CD^2 = {{d}}^2 + {{ad}}^2 - 2 \cdot {{d}} \cdot {{ad}} \cdot \cos{{ang}}°\)

Step 3: \(BD = \sqrt{h^2 + AD^2}\) (if needed directly).

Alternatively, use Pythagoras in the vertical plane through \(B\) and \(D\).
hints: Find the tower height from the angle of elevation.
Find ground distances using the cosine rule.
Use Pythagoras in 3D: \(BD^2 = h^2 + AD^2\).

subject: math_aa
title: Magnitude of a Vector
stem_template: Find the magnitude of \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} {{a}} \\ {{b}} \\ {{c}} \end{pmatrix}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Unit Vector
stem_template: Find the unit vector in the direction of \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} {{a}} \\ {{b}} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\) where \({{a}}^2 + {{b}}^2 = {{c2}}\).
mark_scheme_template: \(|\mathbf{v}| = \sqrt{{{c2}}} = {{c}}\). Unit vector \(\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{1}{{{c}}}\begin{pmatrix}{{a}}\\{{b}}\\0\end{pmatrix}\).
hints: Unit vector \(= \dfrac{\mathbf{v}}{|\mathbf{v}|}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Vector Addition and Position Vectors
stem_template:
Points \(A\) and \(B\) have position vectors \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}\).

**(a)** Find \(\overrightarrow{AB}\).

**(b)** Find the midpoint \(M\) of \(AB\).

**(c)** Find \(|\overrightarrow{AB}|\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1-a1}}\\{{b2-a2}}\\{{b3-a3}}\end{pmatrix}\)

**(b)** \(M = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) = \begin{pmatrix}\frac{{{a1+b1}}}{2}\\\frac{{{a2+b2}}}{2}\\\frac{{{a3+b3}}}{2}\end{pmatrix}\)

**(c)** \(|\overrightarrow{AB}| = \sqrt{({{b1-a1}})^2+({{b2-a2}})^2+({{b3-a3}})^2}\)
hints: \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}\).
Midpoint: average of position vectors.
Magnitude: \(\sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2+v_3^2}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Ratio Point on a Line Segment
stem_template: Point \(P\) divides \(AB\) in the ratio \({{m}}:{{n}}\). Given \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}\), find the position vector of \(P\).
mark_scheme_template:
Section formula: \(\mathbf{p} = \dfrac{n \cdot \mathbf{a} + m \cdot \mathbf{b}}{m+n}\).

\(\mathbf{p} = \dfrac{{{n}}\begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix} + {{m}}\begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}}{{{m+n}}} = \dfrac{1}{{{m+n}}}\begin{pmatrix}{{n*a1+m*b1}}\\{{n*a2+m*b2}}\\{{n*a3+m*b3}}\end{pmatrix}\)
hints: \(P\) divides \(AB\) in ratio \(m:n\) means \(\mathbf{p} = \frac{n\mathbf{a}+m\mathbf{b}}{m+n}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Dot Product Calculation
stem_template: Find \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}\) where \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}\).
hints: \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3\)

subject: math_aa
title: Angle Between Two Vectors
stem_template: Find the angle between \(\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix}{{u1}}\\{{u2}}\\{{u3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix}{{v1}}\\{{v2}}\\{{v3}}\end{pmatrix}\), giving your answer in degrees to 1 decimal place.
mark_scheme_template:
\(\cos\theta = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{|\mathbf{u}||\mathbf{v}|}\)

\(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = {{u1*v1+u2*v2+u3*v3}}\)

\(|\mathbf{u}| = \sqrt{{{u1*u1+u2*u2+u3*u3}}}\), \(|\mathbf{v}| = \sqrt{{{v1*v1+v2*v2+v3*v3}}}\)

\(\theta = \arccos\left(\dfrac{{{u1*v1+u2*v2+u3*v3}}}{\sqrt{{{u1*u1+u2*u2+u3*u3}}} \cdot \sqrt{{{v1*v1+v2*v2+v3*v3}}}}\right)\)
hints: Use \(\cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{|\mathbf{u}||\mathbf{v}|}\).
Compute the dot product and both magnitudes separately.

subject: math_aa
title: Perpendicular Vectors
stem_template: Find the value of \(k\) such that \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\k\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular.
mark_scheme_template:
For perpendicular vectors: \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = 0\).

\({{a1}} \cdot {{b1}} + {{a2}} \cdot {{b2}} + k \cdot {{b3}} = 0\)
\({{a1*b1 + a2*b2}} + {{b3}}k = 0\)
\(k = -\dfrac{{{a1*b1 + a2*b2}}}{{{b3}}}\)
hints: Perpendicular vectors satisfy \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=0\).
Set up the equation and solve for \(k\).

subject: math_aa
title: Perpendicularity Check
stem_template: Are \(\mathbf{u} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a}}\\{{b}}\\{{c}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix}{{p}}\\{{q}}\\{{r}}\end{pmatrix}\) perpendicular?

options_template.text: Only if they are unit vectors

mark_scheme_template: \(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = {{a*p}} + {{b*q}} + {{c}}\cdot {{r}} = {{a*p+b*q+c*r}} = 0\). Yes, they are perpendicular.
hints: Compute the dot product. If it equals 0, the vectors are perpendicular.

subject: math_aa
title: Vector Equation of a Line
stem_template: A line passes through \(A({{a1}}, {{a2}}, {{a3}})\) with direction vector \(\mathbf{d} = \begin{pmatrix}{{d1}}\\{{d2}}\\{{d3}}\end{pmatrix}\). Write the vector equation of the line.
mark_scheme_template: Vector equation: \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}{{d1}}\\{{d2}}\\{{d3}}\end{pmatrix}\), \(t \in \mathbb{R}\).
hints: Vector equation of a line: \(\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{a} + t\mathbf{d}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Parametric and Cartesian Forms of a Line
stem_template:
A line passes through \(A({{a1}}, {{a2}}, {{a3}})\) and \(B({{b1}}, {{b2}}, {{b3}})\).

**(a)** Find the direction vector \(\overrightarrow{AB}\).

**(b)** Write the line in vector form and parametric form.

**(c)** Convert to Cartesian (symmetric) form.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1-a1}}\\{{b2-a2}}\\{{b3-a3}}\end{pmatrix}\)

**(b)** Vector: \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}{{b1-a1}}\\{{b2-a2}}\\{{b3-a3}}\end{pmatrix}\)

Parametric: \(x = {{a1}} + {{b1-a1}}t\), \(y = {{a2}} + {{b2-a2}}t\), \(z = {{a3}} + {{b3-a3}}t\)

**(c)** \(\dfrac{x-{{a1}}}{{{b1-a1}}} = \dfrac{y-{{a2}}}{{{b2-a2}}} = \dfrac{z-{{a3}}}{{{b3-a3}}}\)
hints: Direction vector: \(\mathbf{d} = B - A\).
Parametric form: express each coordinate using parameter \(t\).
Cartesian form: set each fraction equal (eliminate \(t\)).

subject: math_aa
title: Point on a Line and Distance
stem_template:
Line \(L\): \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}{{d1}}\\{{d2}}\\{{d3}}\end{pmatrix}\).

**(a)** Does \(P({{p1}}, {{p2}}, {{p3}})\) lie on \(L\)?

**(b)** Find the foot of the perpendicular from \(Q({{q1}}, {{q2}}, {{q3}})\) to \(L\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Check if \(\begin{pmatrix}{{p1-a1}}\\{{p2-a2}}\\{{p3-a3}}\end{pmatrix}\) is a scalar multiple of \(\mathbf{d}\). Since \({{p1-a1}}/{{d1}} = {{t0}}\) for all components, \(P\) lies on \(L\) with \(t={{t0}}\).

**(b)** Let \(F = \mathbf{a} + t\mathbf{d}\) be the foot. \(\overrightarrow{QF} = F - Q\) must be perpendicular to \(\mathbf{d}\):
\(\overrightarrow{QF} \cdot \mathbf{d} = 0\). Solve for \(t\), then find \(F\).
hints: For (a): Try to find \(t\) such that \(A + t\mathbf{d} = P\); must be consistent.
For (b): The foot satisfies \(\overrightarrow{QF} \cdot \mathbf{d} = 0\).

subject: math_aa
title: Intersecting and Skew Lines
stem_template:
Two lines are given:
\(L_1: \mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix} + s\begin{pmatrix}{{d1}}\\{{d2}}\\{{d3}}\end{pmatrix}\)
\(L_2: \mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}{{e1}}\\{{e2}}\\{{e3}}\end{pmatrix}\)

Determine whether the lines are parallel, intersecting, or skew.
mark_scheme_template:
Step 1: Check if direction vectors are parallel (one a scalar multiple of the other).

Step 2: If not parallel, set \(L_1 = L_2\) and solve the system for \(s, t\). Check consistency:
- Consistent: lines intersect, state the point.
- Inconsistent: lines are skew (non-parallel, non-intersecting).
hints: First check if direction vectors are proportional (parallel lines).
If not parallel, attempt to solve for parameters \(s\) and \(t\).
Check all three component equations — if the system is inconsistent, the lines are skew.

subject: math_aa
title: Shortest Distance Between Skew Lines
stem_template: Find the shortest distance between the skew lines \(L_1: \mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix} + s\begin{pmatrix}{{d1}}\\{{d2}}\\{{d3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(L_2: \mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}{{e1}}\\{{e2}}\\{{e3}}\end{pmatrix}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Shortest distance \(= \dfrac{|(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}) \cdot (\mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2)|}{|\mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2|}\)

Step 1: Compute \(\mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2 = \mathbf{n}\) (common perpendicular).
Step 2: Compute \((\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}) \cdot \mathbf{n}\).
Step 3: Divide by \(|\mathbf{n}|\).
hints: The shortest distance uses the formula \(d = \frac{|(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a})\cdot(\mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2)|}{|\mathbf{d}_1 \times \mathbf{d}_2|}\).
First compute the cross product of the direction vectors.

subject: math_aa
title: Parallel Lines
stem_template: Are the lines \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix} + s\begin{pmatrix}{{a}}\\{{b}}\\{{c}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}4\\5\\6\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}{{2*a}}\\{{2*b}}\\{{2*c}}\end{pmatrix}\) parallel?

options_template.text: Yes — the direction vectors are proportional
No — direction vectors must be identical
Only if the lines are coincident
Cannot determine without checking a point

mark_scheme_template: The second direction vector \(\begin{pmatrix}{{2*a}}\\{{2*b}}\\{{2*c}}\end{pmatrix} = 2\begin{pmatrix}{{a}}\\{{b}}\\{{c}}\end{pmatrix}\) is a scalar multiple of the first. So the lines are **parallel** (but check if they coincide by seeing if the connecting vector is also parallel).
hints: Lines are parallel when their direction vectors are scalar multiples of each other.

subject: math_aa
title: Cross Product Calculation
stem_template: Find \(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}\) where \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}\). Verify that the result is perpendicular to both \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\).
mark_scheme_template:
\(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = \begin{vmatrix}\mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\ {{a1}} & {{a2}} & {{a3}} \\ {{b1}} & {{b2}} & {{b3}}\end{vmatrix}\)

\(= \mathbf{i}({{a2*b3 - a3*b2}}) - \mathbf{j}({{a1*b3 - a3*b1}}) + \mathbf{k}({{a1*b2 - a2*b1}})\)

\(= \begin{pmatrix}{{a2*b3-a3*b2}}\\{{a3*b1-a1*b3}}\\{{a1*b2-a2*b1}}\end{pmatrix}\)

Verify: \(\mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}) = 0\) and \(\mathbf{b} \cdot (\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}) = 0\) ✓
hints: \(\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b} = \begin{vmatrix}\mathbf{i}&\mathbf{j}&\mathbf{k}\\a_1&a_2&a_3\\b_1&b_2&b_3\end{vmatrix}\)
Verify using dot products with both original vectors.

subject: math_aa
title: Area of Parallelogram Using Cross Product
stem_template: Find the area of the parallelogram with adjacent sides \(\overrightarrow{OA} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\overrightarrow{OB} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Area \(= |\overrightarrow{OA} \times \overrightarrow{OB}|\).

\(\overrightarrow{OA}\times\overrightarrow{OB} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a2*b3-a3*b2}}\\{{a3*b1-a1*b3}}\\{{a1*b2-a2*b1}}\end{pmatrix}\)

\(\text{Area} = \sqrt{({{a2*b3-a3*b2}})^2+({{a3*b1-a1*b3}})^2+({{a1*b2-a2*b1}})^2}\)
hints: Area of parallelogram \(= |\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}|\).
Compute the cross product first, then its magnitude.

subject: math_aa
title: Volume of Parallelepiped
stem_template: Find the volume of the parallelepiped with edges \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1}}\\{{b2}}\\{{b3}}\end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{c} = \begin{pmatrix}{{c1}}\\{{c2}}\\{{c3}}\end{pmatrix}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Volume \(= |\mathbf{a}\cdot(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c})|\) (scalar triple product).

\(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b2*c3-b3*c2}}\\{{b3*c1-b1*c3}}\\{{b1*c2-b2*c1}}\end{pmatrix}\)

\(\mathbf{a}\cdot(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c}) = {{a1}}({{b2*c3-b3*c2}}) - {{a2}}({{b1*c3-b3*c1}}) + {{a3}}({{b1*c2-b2*c1}})\)

Volume \(= |\text{result}|\)
hints: Volume \(= |\mathbf{a}\cdot(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c})|\).
Alternatively, compute the determinant of the \(3\times 3\) matrix with \(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}\) as rows.

subject: math_aa
title: Equation of a Plane
stem_template:
A plane passes through \(A({{a1}}, {{a2}}, {{a3}})\) with normal vector \(\mathbf{n} = \begin{pmatrix}{{n1}}\\{{n2}}\\{{n3}}\end{pmatrix}\).

**(a)** Write the equation of the plane in vector form.

**(b)** Write the Cartesian equation.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{n} = \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{n}\)

\(d = {{a1*n1 + a2*n2 + a3*n3}}\)

\(\mathbf{r} \cdot \begin{pmatrix}{{n1}}\\{{n2}}\\{{n3}}\end{pmatrix} = {{a1*n1+a2*n2+a3*n3}}\)

**(b)** Cartesian: \({{n1}}x + {{n2}}y + {{n3}}z = {{a1*n1+a2*n2+a3*n3}}\)
hints: Vector equation of plane: \(\mathbf{r}\cdot\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{n}\).
Expand the dot product to get the Cartesian form.

subject: math_aa
title: Plane Through Three Points
stem_template: Find the equation of the plane passing through \(A({{a1}},{{a2}},{{a3}})\), \(B({{b1}},{{b2}},{{b3}})\), \(C({{c1}},{{c2}},{{c3}})\).
mark_scheme_template:
Step 1: Find two vectors in the plane:
\(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix}{{b1-a1}}\\{{b2-a2}}\\{{b3-a3}}\end{pmatrix}\), \(\overrightarrow{AC} = \begin{pmatrix}{{c1-a1}}\\{{c2-a2}}\\{{c3-a3}}\end{pmatrix}\)

Step 2: Find normal \(\mathbf{n} = \overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{AC}\).

Step 3: Plane equation: \(\mathbf{n}\cdot(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{a}) = 0\), giving the Cartesian form.
hints: Find two vectors lying in the plane.
Normal vector \(= \) cross product of these two vectors.
Use point-normal form: \(\mathbf{n}\cdot(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{a})=0\).

subject: math_aa
title: Distance from a Point to a Plane
stem_template: Find the perpendicular distance from \(P({{p1}}, {{p2}}, {{p3}})\) to the plane \({{n1}}x + {{n2}}y + {{n3}}z = {{d}}\).
hints: Distance formula: \(\frac{|\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{p} - d|}{|\mathbf{n}|}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Intersection of Line and Plane
stem_template: Find the point of intersection of the line \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}{{a1}}\\{{a2}}\\{{a3}}\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}{{d1}}\\{{d2}}\\{{d3}}\end{pmatrix}\) and the plane \({{n1}}x + {{n2}}y + {{n3}}z = {{D}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Substitute parametric equations into plane equation:
\({{n1}}({{a1}} + {{d1}}t) + {{n2}}({{a2}} + {{d2}}t) + {{n3}}({{a3}} + {{d3}}t) = {{D}}\)

\(({{n1*a1+n2*a2+n3*a3}}) + ({{n1*d1+n2*d2+n3*d3}})t = {{D}}\)

\(t = \dfrac{{{D}} - {{n1*a1+n2*a2+n3*a3}}}{{{n1*d1+n2*d2+n3*d3}}}\)

Substitute \(t\) back to find the intersection point.
hints: Substitute \(x = a_1+d_1t\), \(y = a_2+d_2t\), \(z=a_3+d_3t\) into the plane equation.
Solve for \(t\), then find the coordinates.

subject: math_aa
title: Angle Between Two Planes
stem_template: Find the acute angle between the planes \(\Pi_1: {{n1}}x + {{n2}}y + {{n3}}z = {{d1}}\) and \(\Pi_2: {{m1}}x + {{m2}}y + {{m3}}z = {{d2}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
The angle between planes equals the angle between their normals \(\mathbf{n}_1 = ({{n1}},{{n2}},{{n3}})\) and \(\mathbf{n}_2 = ({{m1}},{{m2}},{{m3}})\).

\(\cos\theta = \dfrac{|\mathbf{n}_1 \cdot \mathbf{n}_2|}{|\mathbf{n}_1||\mathbf{n}_2|} = \dfrac{|{{n1*m1+n2*m2+n3*m3}}|}{\sqrt{{{n1*n1+n2*n2+n3*n3}}}\cdot\sqrt{{{m1*m1+m2*m2+m3*m3}}}}\)

\(\theta = \arccos(\cdots)\) degrees.
hints: The angle between planes equals the angle between their normal vectors.
Use the dot product formula: \(\cos\theta = \frac{|\mathbf{n}_1\cdot\mathbf{n}_2|}{|\mathbf{n}_1||\mathbf{n}_2|}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Intersection of Two Planes
stem_template: Find the line of intersection of the planes \({{n1}}x + {{n2}}y + {{n3}}z = {{d1}}\) and \({{m1}}x + {{m2}}y + {{m3}}z = {{d2}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
Step 1: Direction of intersection line \(= \mathbf{n}_1 \times \mathbf{n}_2\).

\(\mathbf{d} = \begin{pmatrix}{{n1}}\\{{n2}}\\{{n3}}\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}{{m1}}\\{{m2}}\\{{m3}}\end{pmatrix}\)

Step 2: Find a particular point by setting one coordinate to 0 and solving the \(2\times 2\) system.

Step 3: Write the line as \(\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{p} + t\mathbf{d}\).
hints: Direction of intersection = cross product of the two normal vectors.
Find a point by setting, say, \(z=0\) and solving the two plane equations simultaneously.

subject: math_aa
title: Mean of a Data Set
stem_template: Find the mean of the data set: \(\{{{v1}}, {{v2}}, {{v3}}, {{v4}}, {{v5}}\}\).
hints: Mean \(= \frac{\text{sum of values}}{\text{number of values}}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Standard Deviation Interpretation
stem_template: Two classes have the same mean score of 65. Class A has standard deviation 3, Class B has standard deviation 12. Which class has more consistent scores?

options_template.text: Class A (smaller SD means less spread)
Class B (larger SD means more spread)
Cannot determine without the full data

mark_scheme_template: Standard deviation measures spread. Smaller SD \(\Rightarrow\) more consistent. Class A (SD=3) is more consistent than Class B (SD=12).
hints: Smaller standard deviation means the data is clustered more closely around the mean.

subject: math_aa
title: Mean and Standard Deviation from Frequency Table
stem_template:
The following frequency table shows the scores of {{n}} students.

| Score | Frequency |
|---|---|
| {{s1}} | {{f1}} |
| {{s2}} | {{f2}} |
| {{s3}} | {{f3}} |
| {{s4}} | {{f4}} |

Find **(a)** the mean score and **(b)** the standard deviation.
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i}{\sum f_i} = \dfrac{{{s1*f1+s2*f2+s3*f3+s4*f4}}}{{{f1+f2+f3+f4}}}\)

**(b)** \(\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum f_i x_i^2}{\sum f_i} - \bar{x}^2\)

\(\sum f_i x_i^2 = {{f1*s1*s1+f2*s2*s2+f3*s3*s3+f4*s4*s4}}\)

\(\sigma = \sqrt{\text{variance}}\)
hints: Weighted mean: \(\bar{x} = \frac{\sum fx}{\sum f}\).
Variance: \(\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum fx^2}{\sum f} - \bar{x}^2\).

subject: math_aa
title: Quartiles and Interquartile Range
stem_template:
The data set is: \(\{{{data}}\}\) (in ascending order).

**(a)** Find \(Q_1\), \(Q_2\), \(Q_3\).

**(b)** Find the interquartile range.

**(c)** Identify any outliers using the criterion \(x < Q_1 - 1.5 \times IQR\) or \(x > Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR\).
mark_scheme_template:
With 8 data values:
\(Q_2\) = median = mean of 4th and 5th values \(= \frac{{{d4+d5}}}{2}\).
\(Q_1\) = median of lower half \(\{{{d1}},{{d2}},{{d3}},{{d4}}\}\) = mean of 2nd and 3rd = \(\frac{{{d2+d3}}}{2}\).
\(Q_3\) = median of upper half = \(\frac{{{d6+d7}}}{2}\).

\(IQR = Q_3 - Q_1\).

Outlier bounds: lower \(= Q_1 - 1.5 \times IQR\), upper \(= Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR\). Check each value.
hints: Split data into lower and upper halves around the median.
\(Q_1\) = median of lower half, \(Q_3\) = median of upper half.

subject: math_aa
title: Complementary Events
stem_template: The probability of event \(A\) is \(P(A) = \dfrac{{{p}}}{{{q}}}\). Find \(P(A')\).

subject: math_aa
title: Sample Space and Probability
stem_template:
A bag contains \({{r}}\) red, \({{b}}\) blue, and \({{g}}\) green balls. One ball is drawn at random.

**(a)** Find \(P(\text{red})\).

**(b)** Find \(P(\text{not blue})\).

**(c)** Find \(P(\text{red or green})\).
mark_scheme_template:
Total balls \(= {{r+b+g}}\).

**(a)** \(P(\text{red}) = \dfrac{{{r}}}{{{r+b+g}}}\)

**(b)** \(P(\text{not blue}) = 1 - \dfrac{{{b}}}{{{r+b+g}}} = \dfrac{{{r+g}}}{{{r+b+g}}}\)

**(c)** \(P(\text{red or green}) = \dfrac{{{r}}+{{g}}}{{{r+b+g}}} = \dfrac{{{r+g}}}{{{r+b+g}}}\)
hints: Count favourable outcomes over total outcomes.
For 'not blue', use the complement rule.

subject: math_aa
title: Venn Diagram Probability
stem_template:
In a class of \({{n}}\) students, \({{a}}\) study Maths, \({{b}}\) study Physics, and \({{c}}\) study both. Find:

**(a)** \(P(\text{Maths only})\)

**(b)** \(P(\text{Maths or Physics})\)

**(c)** \(P(\text{neither})\)
hints: Inclusion-exclusion: \(|A\cup B| = |A|+|B|-|A\cap B|\).
Neither = Total \(-|A\cup B|\).

subject: math_aa
title: Addition Rule for Probability
stem_template: Events \(A\) and \(B\) satisfy \(P(A) = {{pa}}\), \(P(B) = {{pb}}\), and they are mutually exclusive. Find \(P(A \cup B)\).
mark_scheme_template: For mutually exclusive events: \(P(A\cup B) = P(A)+P(B) = {{pa}}+{{pb}} = {{pa+pb}}\).
hints: Mutually exclusive: \(P(A\cap B)=0\), so \(P(A\cup B)=P(A)+P(B)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Independence and Multiplication Rule
stem_template:
Events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent with \(P(A) = {{pa}}\) and \(P(B) = {{pb}}\).

**(a)** Find \(P(A \cap B)\).

**(b)** Find \(P(A \cup B)\).

**(c)** Find \(P(A' \cap B)\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** Independent: \(P(A\cap B) = P(A)\cdot P(B) = {{pa}} \times {{pb}} = {{pa*pb}}\)

**(b)** \(P(A\cup B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B) = {{pa}}+{{pb}}-{{pa*pb}} = {{pa+pb-pa*pb}}\)

**(c)** \(P(A'\cap B) = P(B)-P(A\cap B) = {{pb}}-{{pa*pb}} = {{pb*(1-pa)}} = P(A')P(B)\) ✓
hints: Independent: \(P(A\cap B) = P(A)P(B)\).
Addition rule: \(P(A\cup B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A\cap B)\).
\(P(A'\cap B) = P(B) - P(A\cap B)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Testing Independence
stem_template: Given \(P(A) = {{pa}}\), \(P(B) = {{pb}}\), \(P(A\cap B) = {{pab}}\). Are \(A\) and \(B\) independent? Justify your answer.
mark_scheme_template:
Check if \(P(A\cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\).

\(P(A)\cdot P(B) = {{pa}} \times {{pb}} = {{pa*pb}}\).

Given \(P(A\cap B) = {{pab}}\).

Since \({{pab}} {{comparison}} {{pa*pb}}\), the events are **{{conclusion}}**.
hints: Check the independence condition: \(P(A\cap B) = P(A)\cdot P(B)\).
If equal: independent. If not equal: dependent.

subject: math_aa
title: Conditional Probability
mark_scheme_template: \(P(A|B) = \dfrac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)} = \dfrac{{{pab}}}{{{pb}}}\)
hints: \(P(A|B) = \frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Tree Diagram and Conditional Probability
stem_template:
A box contains \({{r}}\) red and \({{b}}\) blue balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement.

**(a)** Draw a tree diagram.

**(b)** Find \(P(\text{both red})\).

**(c)** Find \(P(\text{second red} | \text{first red})\).
hints: Without replacement: after drawing a red ball, the total decreases by 1.
Use the conditional probability on the tree diagram.

subject: math_aa
title: Bayes' Theorem
stem_template:
A factory has two machines. Machine A produces \({{pa}}\%\) of the output with a defect rate of \({{da}}\%\), and machine B produces the rest with a defect rate of \({{db}}\%\).

**(a)** Find \(P(\text{defective})\).

**(b)** Given that an item is defective, find \(P(\text{from machine A})\).
hints: Use the law of total probability for part (a).
Use Bayes' theorem: \(P(A|D) = \frac{P(D|A)P(A)}{P(D)}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Bayes' Theorem: Medical Test
stem_template: A test for a disease has sensitivity \({{sens}}\%\) (true positive rate) and specificity \({{spec}}\%\) (true negative rate). The disease affects \({{prev}}\%\) of the population. A person tests positive. Find the probability they actually have the disease.
mark_scheme_template:
Let \(D\) = has disease, \(T^+\) = positive test.
\(P(D) = {{prev/100}}\), \(P(T^+|D) = {{sens/100}}\), \(P(T^+|D') = 1 - {{spec/100}} = {{(100-spec)/100}}\).

\(P(T^+) = {{sens/100}} \times {{prev/100}} + {{(100-spec)/100}} \times {{(100-prev)/100}}\)

\(P(D|T^+) = \dfrac{{{sens/100}} \times {{prev/100}}}{P(T^+)}\)

Note: even with high sensitivity/specificity, a rare disease has low positive predictive value.
hints: Apply Bayes' theorem: \(P(D|T^+) = \frac{P(T^+|D)P(D)}{P(T^+)}\).
Compute \(P(T^+)\) using the law of total probability.

subject: math_aa
title: Expected Value of Discrete RV
stem_template:
A discrete random variable \(X\) has the following distribution:
\[P(X = {{x1}}) = {{p1}},\quad P(X = {{x2}}) = {{p2}},\quad P(X = {{x3}}) = {{p3}}\]
Find \(E(X)\).
hints: \(E(X) = \sum x_i P(X=x_i)\): multiply each value by its probability and sum.

subject: math_aa
title: Variance of a Discrete RV
stem_template:
A random variable \(X\) has \(E(X) = {{mu}}\) and \(E(X^2) = {{esq}}\).

**(a)** Find \(\text{Var}(X)\).

**(b)** Find \(\text{Var}({{a}}X + {{b}})\).
mark_scheme_template:
**(a)** \(\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 = {{esq}} - {{mu}}^2 = {{esq - mu*mu}} = {{var}}\)

**(b)** \(\text{Var}({{a}}X+{{b}}) = {{a}}^2 \text{Var}(X) = {{a*a}} \times {{var}} = {{a*a*var}}\)

(Adding a constant does not change variance.)
hints: \(\text{Var}(aX+b) = a^2\text{Var}(X)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Full Probability Distribution Table
stem_template: A fair die is rolled. Let \(X\) be the score modulo \({{m}}\) (i.e., \(X = \text{score} \mod {{m}}\)). Construct the probability distribution of \(X\) and find \(E(X)\) and \(\text{Var}(X)\).
mark_scheme_template:
For \(m = {{m}}\):

Values of \(X\): \(0, 1, \ldots, {{m-1}}\)

Count how many die scores give each value (scores 1-6):
\(P(X=k) = \) count/6 for each \(k\).

Compute \(E(X) = \sum k \cdot P(X=k)\) and \(\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\).
hints: For each possible value of \(X\), count how many of \{1,2,3,4,5,6\} give that remainder when divided by \(m\).
Use \(E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2\) for variance.

subject: math_aa
title: Binomial Probability
hints: Binomial formula: \(P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Binomial Mean and Variance
hints: For \(P(X\geq k)\), use complement: \(1 - P(X\leq k-1)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Poisson Distribution
stem_template:
Errors occur in a document at a mean rate of \({{lam}}\) per page. Assuming a Poisson distribution:

**(a)** Find the probability of exactly \({{k}}\) errors on a page.

**(b)** Find the probability of fewer than \({{m}}\) errors on a page.
hints: Poisson formula: \(P(X=k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}\).
For cumulative probabilities, sum individual terms.

subject: math_aa
title: Binomial vs Poisson Approximation
stem_template: A sample of {{n}} items is tested. The probability that any item is defective is \(p = \frac{1}{{{k}}}\). Compare the exact binomial probability \(P(X={{r}})\) with the Poisson approximation.
mark_scheme_template:
Exact: \(X \sim B\!\left({{n}}, \frac{1}{{{k}}}\right)\). \(P(X={{r}}) = \binom{{{n}}}{{{r}}}\!\left(\frac{1}{{{k}}}\right)^{{{r}}}\!\left(1-\frac{1}{{{k}}}\right)^{{{n-r}}}\)

Poisson approximation: \(\lambda = np = \frac{{{n}}}{{{k}}}\). \(P(X={{r}}) \approx \frac{e^{-{{n/k}}}\left(\frac{{{n}}}{{{k}}}\right)^{{{r}}}}{{{r}}!}\)

For large \(n\) and small \(p\), both give similar results.
hints: Poisson approximation is valid when \(n\) is large, \(p\) is small, and \(\lambda = np\) is moderate.
Compute both values and compare.

subject: math_aa
title: Normal Distribution Z-Score
stem_template: \(X \sim N({{mu}}, {{sigma}}^2)\). Find the \(z\)-score for \(X = {{x}}\).
mark_scheme_template: \(z = \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma} = \dfrac{{{x}}-{{mu}}}{{{sigma}}} = \dfrac{{{x-mu}}}{{{sigma}}}\)
hints: \(z = \frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Normal Probability Calculations
stem_template:
The heights of students are normally distributed with mean \({{mu}}\) cm and standard deviation \({{sigma}}\) cm. Find:

**(a)** \(P(X > {{x1}})\)

**(b)** \(P({{x2}} < X < {{x3}})\)

**(c)** The height \(h\) such that \(P(X < h) = {{q}}\).
hints: Standardise using \(z = \frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\).
Use the standard normal table or GDC.
For inverse normal, use \(x = \mu + z\sigma\).

subject: math_aa
title: Finding Normal Parameters
stem_template: \(X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\). Given \(P(X < {{a}}) = {{p1}}\) and \(P(X > {{b}}) = {{p2}}\), find \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).
mark_scheme_template:
From \(P(X < {{a}}) = {{p1}}\): \(\frac{{{a}}-\mu}{\sigma} = z_1\) where \(z_1 = \Phi^{-1}({{p1}})\) (negative).

From \(P(X > {{b}}) = {{p2}}\): \(\frac{{{b}}-\mu}{\sigma} = z_2\) where \(z_2 = \Phi^{-1}(1-{{p2}})\) (positive).

Two equations:
\({{a}} - \mu = z_1 \sigma\)
\({{b}} - \mu = z_2 \sigma\)

Subtract: \({{b-a}} = (z_2-z_1)\sigma\), so \(\sigma = \frac{{{b-a}}}{z_2-z_1}\). Then find \(\mu\).
hints: Convert each probability condition to a \(z\)-score equation.
Solve the two simultaneous equations for \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).

subject: math_aa
title: Normal Approximation to Binomial
stem_template: \(X \sim B({{n}}, {{p}})\). Use the normal approximation (with continuity correction) to find \(P(X \geq {{k}})\).
mark_scheme_template:
Check: \(np = {{n*p}}\) and \(n(1-p) = {{n*(1-p)}}\) are both \(> 5\), so approximation is valid.

\(\mu = np = {{n*p}}\), \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{{{n*p*(1-p)}}}\)

With continuity correction: \(P(X \geq {{k}}) \approx P\!\left(Y > {{k}}-0.5\right)\) where \(Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\).

\(z = \frac{{{k-0.5}} - {{n*p}}}{\sqrt{{{n*p*(1-p)}}}}\), then \(P(Z > z)\).
hints: Normal approx to binomial: \(\mu = np\), \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\).
Continuity correction: \(P(X\geq k) \approx P(Y > k-0.5)\).

subject: math_aa
title: Limit of a Polynomial
stem_template: Find \(\displaystyle\lim_{x \to {{a}}} ({{b}}x^2 + {{c}}x + {{d}})\).
mark_scheme_template:
For a polynomial, the limit is found by direct substitution:
\(\lim_{x\to{{a}}} ({{b}}x^2 + {{c}}x + {{d}}) = {{b}}({{a}})^2 + {{c}}({{a}}) + {{d}} = {{b*a*a + c*a + d}}\)
hints: For a polynomial, substitute \(x = {{a}}\) directly.

subject: math_aa
title: Derivative from First Principles
stem_template: Using the definition \(f'(x) = \displaystyle\lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}\), find \(f'(x)\) for \(f(x) = {{a}}x^2 + {{b}}x\).
hints: Expand \(f(x+h)\), subtract \(f(x)\), divide by \(h\), then take the limit as \(h\to 0\).
Terms without \(h\) will cancel; remaining terms divide by \(h\).

subject: math_aa
title: Continuity and Limit
stem_template: Find \(\displaystyle\lim_{x \to {{a}}} \dfrac{x^2 - {{a2}}}{x - {{a}}}\) and state whether the function \(f(x) = \dfrac{x^2-{{a2}}}{x-{{a}}}\) is continuous at \(x = {{a}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
\(\frac{x^2 - {{a2}}}{x-{{a}}} = \frac{(x-{{a}})(x+{{a}})}{x-{{a}}} = x + {{a}}\) for \(x \neq {{a}}\).

\(\lim_{x\to{{a}}} (x+{{a}}) = 2 \times {{a}} = {{2*a}}\).

Since \(f({{a}})\) is undefined (\(0/0\)), \(f\) is not continuous at \(x={{a}}\). The limit exists but the function has a removable discontinuity.
hints: Factor the numerator as a difference of squares: \(x^2 - a^2 = (x-a)(x+a)\).
Cancel the common factor, then take the limit.

subject: math_aa
title: First Principles for Exponential
stem_template: Use first principles to find the derivative of \(f(x) = e^x\), using the limit \(\displaystyle\lim_{h\to 0}\dfrac{e^h-1}{h} = 1\).
mark_scheme_template: \(f'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \frac{e^{x+h}-e^x}{h} = \lim_{h\to 0} \frac{e^x(e^h-1)}{h} = e^x \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{e^h-1}{h} = e^x \times 1 = e^x\)
hints: Factor out \(e^x\) from the numerator.
Use the given limit \(\lim_{h\to 0}\frac{e^h-1}{h} = 1\).

subject: math_aa
title: Power Rule
stem_template: Find \(\dfrac{d}{dx}\left({{a}}x^{{n}} + {{b}}x + {{c}}\right)\).
hints: Power rule: \(\frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}\).
The derivative of a constant is 0.

subject: math_aa
title: Chain Rule
stem_template: Find \(\dfrac{d}{dx}\left({{a}}x + {{b}}\right)^{{n}}\).
hints: Chain rule: \(\frac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x))\cdot g'(x)\).
The 'inner function' is \({{a}}x+{{b}}\) with derivative \({{a}}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Product and Quotient Rules
hints: Product rule: \((uv)' = u'v + uv'\).
Quotient rule: \(\left(\frac{u}{v}\right)' = \frac{u'v-uv'}{v^2}\).

subject: math_aa
title: Implicit Differentiation
stem_template: Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) for the curve \(x^2 + {{a}}xy + y^2 = {{k}}\) by implicit differentiation.
mark_scheme_template:
Differentiate both sides with respect to \(x\):
\(2x + {{a}}(y + x\frac{dy}{dx}) + 2y\frac{dy}{dx} = 0\)

\(2x + {{a}}y + ({{a}}x + 2y)\frac{dy}{dx} = 0\)

\(\frac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{2x + {{a}}y}{{{a}}x + 2y}\)
hints: Differentiate each term with respect to \(x\), using the chain rule for terms involving \(y\).
Remember: \(\frac{d}{dx}(y^2) = 2y\frac{dy}{dx}\) and \(\frac{d}{dx}(xy) = y + x\frac{dy}{dx}\).
Collect terms with \(\frac{dy}{dx}\) and solve.

subject: math_aa
title: Finding Stationary Points
stem_template: Find the \(x\)-coordinates of the stationary points of \(f(x) = {{a}}x^3 + {{b}}x^2 + {{c}}x + {{d}}\).
mark_scheme_template:
